title: Good: secure_zero() uses volatile pointer and memory barrier
description:
Location: lichen_util.h:24-31

The secure_zero() implementation is correct:
1. volatile uint8_t *p prevents the compiler from eliding the stores
2. __asm__ __volatile__('' ::: 'memory') prevents LTO from removing the function and ensures ordering

Why this is good: memset() on dead buffers is commonly optimized away. Many 'secure memset' implementations miss the LTO case or use compiler-specific pragmas. This portable approach (volatile + barrier) will survive aggressive optimization without relying on __attribute__((noinline)) or similar. Worth preserving if someone suggests 'just use memset'.
